summary:
Catherine the Great (r. 1762–1796 AD) was the longest-reigning female ruler in Russian history and one of the most capable monarchs of the 18th century, who seized the throne in a palace coup against her inept husband Peter III, transformed Russia into a major European power through military expansion and administrative reform, and positioned herself as an enlightened despot corresponding with Voltaire and Diderot while simultaneously tightening serfdom and suppressing the [[event:3281|Pugachev Rebellion]]. A German princess (Sophia of Anhalt-Zerbst) who had converted to Russian Orthodoxy on arriving in Russia as Peter III's bride, Catherine learned to speak Russian fluently, adopted Russian culture enthusiastically, and created a Russian identity for herself that proved more genuinely Russian than her husband's.

Catherine's reign saw enormous Russian territorial expansion: the [[event:2445|Russo-Turkish Wars]] won Russian access to the Black Sea and the Crimea (annexed 1783), establishing Russia's position as a Mediterranean power; the [[event:2334|Partitions of Poland]] gave Russia vast Polish territories; and continued expansion in the Caucasus and Central Asia extended Russian dominion further south and east. She transformed St. Petersburg into a genuinely European cultural capital, founded schools, reorganized provincial administration, and assembled one of the world's great art collections (the Hermitage). Her 'Nakaz' (Instruction) of 1767, drawing heavily on Montesquieu and Beccaria, proposed liberal legal reforms — but was largely theoretical; in practice, after the Pugachev Rebellion's demonstration of serf discontent, Catherine strengthened nobles' control over serfs and postponed reform. She represented the paradox of Enlightened absolutism: sincerely committed to Enlightenment principles in theory, constrained by political realities in practice.
